secondary_meaning: a North American country and member of the Commonwealth
usage_notes: Proper noun, always masculine. Often used with article 'le'.
common_collocations: au Canada, le Canada, pour le Canada
image_context: Canadian flag with maple leaf in autumn colors
example_1: Le Canada est un grand pays d'Amérique du Nord.
example_1_translation: Canada is a large country in North America.
example_2_translation: She wants to live in Canada.
pronunciation_tips: Sounds like 'kah-nah-dah'. Stress on first syllable.
memory_hook: Canada sounds the same in French and English!

secondary_meaning: a country and continent in the Southern Hemisphere
usage_notes: Proper noun, always feminine. Use 'en Australie' for 'in Australia'.
image_context: Sydney Opera House and Australian landscape with koala
example_1: L'Australie est connue pour ses kangourous.
example_1_translation: Australia is known for its kangaroos.
example_2: Il a visité l'Australie l'année dernière.
example_3: L'Australie est très loin de l'Europe.
example_3_translation: Australia is very far from Europe.
pronunciation_tips: Sounds like 'os-trah-lee'. Stress on second syllable.
memory_hook: Australia → Australie. Add 'ie' to make it French!

secondary_meaning: a country in Western Europe known for chocolate and beer
usage_notes: Proper noun, always feminine. Use 'en Belgique' for 'in Belgium'.
common_collocations: en Belgique, la Belgique, pour la Belgique
image_context: Belgian flag with Brussels landmark and waffles
example_1: La Belgique est célèbre pour son chocolat.
example_1_translation: Belgium is famous for its chocolate.
example_2: Bruxelles est la capitale de la Belgique.
example_2_translation: Brussels is the capital of Belgium.
example_3: La Belgique a trois langues officielles.
pronunciation_tips: Sounds like 'bell-zhik'. The 'g' before 'i' sounds like 'zh'.
common_mistakes: Remember it's feminine (la Belgique, en Belgique)

secondary_meaning: a country in Central Europe known for mountains, watches, and chocolate
usage_notes: Proper noun, always feminine. Use 'en Suisse' for 'in Switzerland'.
common_collocations: en Suisse, la Suisse, pour la Suisse
image_context: Swiss Alps mountains with Swiss flag and chocolate
example_1: La Suisse a quatre langues officielles.
example_2: Il fait froid en Suisse en hiver.
example_2_translation: It is cold in Switzerland in winter.
example_3: La Suisse est célèbre pour ses montagnes.
example_3_translation: Switzerland is famous for its mountains.
memory_hook: Suisse = sweet? No, but sounds similar! Swiss chocolates are sweet.
common_mistakes: Remember it's feminine (la Suisse, en Suisse)

secondary_meaning: a professional who designs buildings and structures
usage_notes: Can be masculine or feminine. B1 vocabulary for professions.
common_collocations: architecte intérieur, architecte en chef, profession d'architecte
image_context: Architect drawing blueprints at desk with building model
example_2: L'architecte a conçu un bâtiment moderne.
example_2_translation: The architect designed a modern building.
pronunciation_tips: Stress on last: ar-chi-TEK. The 'ch' sounds like 'sh' in French.
memory_hook: Architect → architecte. Same word in both languages!

secondary_meaning: a person who writes or reports news for newspapers, radio, or TV
usage_notes: Can be masculine or feminine. A2 level professional vocabulary.
common_collocations: journaliste en chef, journaliste reporter, profession de journaliste
image_context: Journalist with microphone and press camera
example_1: Elle travaille comme journaliste pour un quotidien.
example_1_translation: She works as a journalist for a daily newspaper.
example_2: Le journaliste a interviewé le président.
example_3: Les journalistes travaillent pour les journaux.
pronunciation_tips: Stress on last: jour-na-LIST. The 'j' sounds like 'zh' in English.

secondary_meaning: a qualified professional who dispenses medicines and health advice
usage_notes: Can be masculine or feminine. Common health profession vocabulary.
common_collocations: pharmacien chef, pharmacien d'officine, consultation avec le pharmacien
image_context: Pharmacist behind pharmacy counter dispensing medicine
example_1: Le pharmacien m'a conseillé ce médicament.
example_1_translation: The pharmacist advised me this medicine.
example_2: Je vais à la pharmacie pour chercher mes médicaments.
example_2_translation: I am going to the pharmacy to pick up my medicine.
pronunciation_tips: Stress on last: phar-ma-SIEN. The 'cie' sounds like 'sya'.

secondary_meaning: a professional who applies scientific knowledge to practical problems
usage_notes: Can be masculine or feminine. B1 vocabulary for professions.
common_collocations: ingénieur civil, ingénieur informatique, ingénieur en chef
image_context: Engineer with hard hat working on construction project
example_1: Mon frère est ingénieur en informatique.
example_1_translation: My brother is a computer engineer.
example_2: L'ingénieur a conçu un pont suspendu.
example_2_translation: The engineer designed a suspension bridge.
example_3: Les ingénieurs travaillent sur ce projet.
example_3_translation: The engineers are working on this project.
pronunciation_tips: Stress on last: in-jé-NIEUR. The 'j' sounds like 'zh'.
memory_hook: Ingénieur = engineer. The 'j' sounds like 'zh' in French.

secondary_meaning: feeling anxious or concerned about something
usage_notes: Changes form: inquiet (masc), inquiète (fem). From Latin 'inquietus'.
common_collocations: très inquiet, être inquiet pour, inquiet de
image_context: Worried person with worried expression and hand on cheek
example_1: Je suis inquiet pour ma santé.
example_1_translation: I am worried about my health.
example_2: Elle paraissait inquiète avant les résultats.
example_2_translation: She seemed worried before the results.
example_3: Ne sois pas inquiet, tout ira bien.
example_3_translation: Don't be worried, everything will be fine.
memory_hook: Inquiet = in + quiet. Not quiet/peaceful, so worried.

secondary_meaning: feeling pleased and satisfied with an achievement or possession
usage_notes: Changes form: fier (masc), fière (fem). Common emotion vocabulary.
common_collocations: très fier, être fier de, fier et courageux
image_context: Proud person with confident smile and arms crossed
example_1: Je suis fier de mes enfants.
example_1_translation: I am proud of my children.
example_2: Elle était fière de son travail.
example_2_translation: She was proud of her work.
memory_hook: Fier sounds like 'fi-air' (flying high with pride).

secondary_meaning: feeling or expressing unhappiness or sorrow
usage_notes: Same form for masculine and feminine. A2 essential emotion vocabulary.
common_collocations: très triste, être triste de, triste à voir
image_context: Sad person with tear and melancholic expression
example_1: Il est triste de ne pas voir ses parents.
example_1_translation: He is sad not to see his parents.
memory_hook: Triste sounds like the English 'tristful' (sadly). Sad mood.
common_mistakes: Same form for male and female (no special feminine form)

secondary_meaning: feeling or showing pleasure and contentment
usage_notes: Changes form: heureux (masc), heureuse (fem). A2 essential vocabulary.
common_collocations: très heureux, être heureux de, heureux comme
image_context: Happy joyful person with big smile and raised hands
example_1: Je suis heureux de vous revoir.
example_1_translation: I am happy to see you again.
example_2: Ils vivent une vie très heureuse.
example_2_translation: They live a very happy life.
pronunciation_tips: Sounds like 'uh-RUH'. The 'eu' sounds like 'uh' in 'duh'.
memory_hook: Heureux = happy. Sounds like 'hour-uh' (all day happiness).

usage_notes: Adverb, does not change. A2 essential time vocabulary.
common_collocations: hier soir, hier matin, hier après-midi
image_context: Calendar showing yesterday's date with retro theme
example_2: Hier soir, nous avons dîné au restaurant.
example_2_translation: Yesterday evening, we dined at the restaurant.
common_mistakes: Do not confuse with 'hière' or add gender/number changes

usage_notes: Adverb, does not change. A2 essential time vocabulary.
common_collocations: demain matin, demain soir, demain après-midi
image_context: Calendar showing tomorrow's date with future theme
example_2: Nous verrons demain si le temps sera beau.
example_2_translation: We will see tomorrow if the weather will be nice.
example_3_translation: What have you planned for tomorrow?
memory_hook: Demain = de + main (de = of, main = hand). The next day by hand.

secondary_meaning: right now; in the present time
usage_notes: Adverb, does not change. FALSE COGNATE: not 'actually'! B1 level.
common_collocations: actuellement en cours, actuellement habiter, travaille actuellement
image_context: Person at present moment with clock showing current time
example_2: Elle travaille actuellement sur un nouveau projet.
example_2_translation: She is currently working on a new project.
example_3: Actuellement, le marché est très actif.
example_3_translation: Currently, the market is very active.
pronunciation_tips: Stress on last: ak-tu-EL-ment. The 'uel' sounds like 'well'.
common_mistakes: DO NOT translate as 'actually'. Use 'en fait' or 'réellement' instead!
